does: This paper introduces REAL, a dataset construction strategy that improves the efficiency and quality of preference annotations for LLM alignment. Rather than annotating all response pairs randomly, REAL selectively identifies and prioritizes less ambiguous preference pairs for human labeling by measuring response similarity through embeddings. The method operates in an off-policy setting, computing response similarities independently of prompts before training begins, avoiding adaptive measurement during the alignment process. The core contribution is demonstrating that this strategic selection of which response pairs to annotate—focusing on dissimilar pairs that are less ambiguous—reduces annotation burden while improving the quality of the resulting preference dataset used in downstream alignment algorithms like DPO.
connects: This work builds directly on the preference-based alignment paradigm established by methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), which rely on curated preference datasets of AI-generated response pairs. It responds to the practical challenge that constructing such datasets is costly and prone to human annotator bias, a well-recognized bottleneck in LLM safety and helpfulness alignment. REAL extends active learning principles and dataset construction literature by applying embedding-based similarity metrics to identify which pairs warrant annotation, drawing on decades of work in optimal experiment design and uncertainty sampling for efficient data acquisition in machine learning.
enables: By reducing annotation costs while maintaining or improving alignment quality, REAL makes large-scale LLM preference training more practical and economically feasible. The approach opens pathways for scaling preference-based alignment to more diverse domains and languages where annotation budgets are limited. Future work could extend the embedding-based selection strategy to other alignment paradigms beyond DPO, develop methods for handling the exploration-exploitation tradeoff in iterative annotation rounds, or combine REAL with active learning approaches that adaptively refine which pairs are most informative. The method also enables investigation of how to systematically reduce human bias in preference annotations through principled pair selection, potentially improving alignment quality across diverse application settings.
